old_claim.note: Early Copyright & Printing Privileges is an earlier historical predecessor or foundation, not a one-to-one engineering dependency.

new_claim.note: Early copyright and printing privileges are the legal-rights predecessor for later recorded-music copyright management; U.S. copyright law later treated musical-composition rights in records and, separately, sound recordings.

source_shape.source_locator: Chapter II explains that the 1909 Act protected musical-composition owners whose works were reproduced in records, while explicit federal protection for sound recordings came later.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The Copyright Office report ties recorded music to copyright-management law for musical compositions and sound recordings.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: This supports a historical-predecessor edge because early copyright privileges are legal-rights ancestry, not the physical recording platform or the first 1889 commercial-production trigger.

invariant_preserved_or_changed: Preserved: early_copyright_printing_privileges remains a historical_predecessor edge, now with source-backed rights-management rationale.
would_reject_if: The node were narrowed to physical cylinder manufacture with copyright management removed from scope.
A stronger source showed early recorded-music firms operated entirely outside copyright or rights-management systems.
The early_copyright_printing_privileges node were narrowed to exclude the legal-rights lineage leading to later copyright law.
short_reason: Copyright privileges are a legal predecessor for recorded-music rights management, not the core recording platform.
